1: Bezalel and Oholiab and every craftsman in whom YHWH put skill and wisdom — Spirit-gifted builders of God's dwelling. The same Spirit who filled these craftsmen for holy construction is poured out on the church for its building: “it was he who gave some to be apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors and teachers, to equip his people for works of service, so that the body of Christ may be built up” (Ephesians 4:11-12). Christ himself is filled with the Spirit “without measure” (John 3:34) — the supreme Spirit-filled builder of God's new temple.
2: Every man whose heart stirred him up to come to do the work. The willing heart drawn by YHWH's initiative: the new-covenant characteristic is the willing heart YHWH gives (Jeremiah 31:33; Ezekiel 36:26-27). Christ's own willingness is the archetype: “I delight to do your will, O my God” (Psalms 40:8; cited in Hebrews 10:7 as Christ's words at the Incarnation). The church's willing service flows from the Spirit who stirs the heart, not from external compulsion.
3: They received from Moses all the freewill offerings the people had brought for the work. The transfer of the community's gifts to the craftsmen who would use them: generosity enabling the construction of God's dwelling. Christ's own self-giving (“he gave himself up for us,” Ephesians 5:25) is the supreme freewill offering that enables the new temple's construction (Ephesians 2:21-22).
4: All the craftsmen came, each from the task he was doing, to report on the work. The organized report from every worker: each contributing from their specific assignment. The body-of-Christ principle: “from him the whole body, joined and held together by every supporting ligament, grows and builds itself up in love, as each part does its work” (Ephesians 4:16).
5: The people are bringing more than enough for the work YHWH commanded. Surplus beyond the need — abundance of offering. The new-covenant parallel: God's grace is never insufficient; it always exceeds the need. “Where sin increased, grace increased all the more” (Romans 5:20). Christ's sacrifice is more than enough: “by one sacrifice he has made perfect forever those who are being made holy” (Hebrews 10:14).
6: Moses commanded: let no man or woman make any more offering for the sanctuary — the offering was sufficient. The declaration of sufficiency — the work has everything it needs. Christ's “It is finished” (John 19:30) is the ultimate declaration: the sacrifice is complete; no further offering is needed. Hebrews 10:18: “where these have been forgiven, sacrifice for sin is no longer necessary.”
7: The material was sufficient and more than enough. The surplus that remained: God's provision is not barely adequate but overflowing. 2 Corinthians 9:8: “God is able to bless you abundantly, so that in all things at all times, having all that you need, you will abound in every good work.” The tabernacle's over-sufficiency is the type of the over-sufficiency of grace in Christ (Ephesians 1:7-8: “the riches of God's grace that he lavished on us”).
8: Every craftsman made the tabernacle with ten curtains of fine twisted linen and blue, purple, and scarlet yarn. The three colors — blue (heavenly), purple (royal), scarlet (sacrificial) — describe Christ in his three offices: prophet (heavenly word), king (royal authority), priest (sacrificial death). The fine linen is associated with righteousness (Revelation 19:8: “the fine linen is the righteous acts of the saints” — derivative of Christ's own righteousness).
9: Each curtain was twenty-eight cubits long and four cubits wide, all curtains the same size. The precise uniformity: each one identical, none greater or lesser. The church's unity: each member equally clothed in the same righteousness of Christ (Galatians 3:28: “there is neither Jew nor Gentile, neither slave nor free, nor is there male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus”). Equal dimensions before God.
10: Five curtains coupled as one set, five as another set. Two groups that must be joined — type of the two peoples (Jew and Gentile) who must be made one. Paul in Ephesians 2:14-16: Christ “has made the two groups one and has destroyed the barrier, the dividing wall of hostility.” The joining of the two curtain-sets is the type of this cosmic reconciliation.
11: Fifty loops of blue on the edge of the outermost curtain of the first set, and fifty loops on the edge of the other, positions matching. The loops are prepared before the clasps: readiness for union before union is achieved. The church is prepared for its eschatological unity through the present work of the Spirit (Ephesians 4:3: “make every effort to keep the unity of the Spirit through the bond of peace”).
12: Fifty loops on one curtain, fifty loops on the edge of the other — the loops opposite one another, even. The perfect alignment of the loops: designed to receive the clasps. Christ's design for his church is a perfect matching: “joined and held together by every supporting ligament” (Ephesians 4:16), each part fitted precisely for the joining.
13: Fifty clasps of gold joining the two sets, so the tabernacle became one whole. Gold clasps joining the two curtain-sets into one: the costly union. The joining of the two into one through the gold of the divine covenant: “His purpose was to create in himself one new humanity out of the two, thus making peace” (Ephesians 2:15).
14: Eleven curtains of goats' hair for the tent over the tabernacle. The outer covering of goat hair: the sacrificial animal as the outer protective layer. The tabernacle's entire structure is covered by what represents the sin-bearing animal (Lev 16). Christ's atonement covers the entire church: “he is the atoning sacrifice for our sins, and not only for ours but also for the sins of the whole world” (1 John 2:2).
15: Each of the eleven curtains the same size — thirty cubits long and four cubits wide. The outer covering is larger than the inner linen curtains (30 vs 28 cubits). The sin-bearing covering exceeds the righteousness-lining: atonement must cover more than mere conformity to righteous standards — it covers the full depth of human need. “Where sin abounded, grace abounded all the more” (Romans 5:20).
16: Five curtains by themselves and six curtains by themselves. The goats' hair curtains divided 5+6 rather than 5+5 as the linen. The asymmetry here may reflect the grace principle: God's provision extends beyond strict equality. Grace is disproportionate — “how much more” (Romans 5:15, 17, 20).
17: Fifty loops on the edge of the outer goats' hair curtain and fifty loops on the other, joining them. The same loop-and-clasp joining principle applied to the outer covering as to the inner linen. The structure of unity is the same at every level: from the innermost linen to the outermost goats' hair, the pattern of prepared loops anticipating joining holds throughout. Christ's atonement creates the same bond at every level of human experience.
18: Fifty bronze clasps joining the tent into one whole. Bronze (judgment) clasps for the outer covering rather than gold. The goats'-hair layer is held together by judgment-metal: Christ's atoning death is a judgment-act (“he condemned sin in the flesh,” Romans 8:3). The bond holding the outer covering together is the same judgment that Christ absorbed at the cross.
19: A covering of rams' skins dyed red, and over that a covering of goatskins. The outermost layers: blood-red rams' skins and durable goatskins. The outermost covering of the tabernacle is blood-red: the dwelling of God is protected by a sacrificial covering that has been through death. Hebrews 10:20: Christ's body is the “curtain” through which we enter — his flesh bore the death that the red rams'-skin covering represents.
20: The frames for the tabernacle of acacia wood, standing upright. The structural skeleton: acacia wood (incorruptible desert wood, a type of the resurrection body) standing vertical. The upright posture is the posture of resurrection: Christ stands risen and exalted (Ephesians 1:20-21: seated at the right hand — the posture of victorious, completed work).
21: Each frame ten cubits tall, one and a half cubits wide. The specific dimensions of the structural members. The Incarnation is specific: the Son of God took a particular human body at a particular time. The tabernacle's measured specificity is the type of the Incarnation's measured specificity (Galatians 4:4: “born of a woman, born under the law”).
22: Two tenons on each frame for joining — so for all the frames of the tabernacle. Two tenons connecting each frame to its foundation: a type of the two natures of Christ joined in one person. The divine and human natures are the two tenons by which the mediator connects heaven (the gold overlay) and earth (the acacia), set firmly in the foundation of redemption (the silver sockets).
23: Twenty frames for the south side of the tabernacle. The precise number determined by the divine blueprint (Exodus 25:9). The church's structure is also divinely determined: “built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, with Christ Jesus himself as the chief cornerstone” (Ephesians 2:20) — not human design but the divine blueprint.
24: Forty silver sockets under the twenty frames, two sockets under each. The silver sockets (adanim, bases) made from the ransom silver of the census (Exodus 30:11-16): the tabernacle literally stands on redemption money. The church stands entirely on the redemption Christ paid: “you were bought at a price” (1 Corinthians 6:20; Exodus 7:23). The foundation is not human achievement but Christ's ransom.
25: Twenty frames for the north side, same construction. The symmetry of south and north: equal dignity, equal foundation. “God does not show favoritism” (Acts 10:34). The church includes all standing on the same silver-of-redemption foundation.
26: Forty silver sockets, two under each frame. The north side mirror of the south: the same redemption-money foundation sustaining every upright member. All stand equally on the full ransom price paid by Christ (Romans 3:22: “there is no difference”).
27: Six frames for the back (west) of the tabernacle. The back wall where the most holy place is located. The west faces away from the sun — the most holy place is not lit by natural light but by the divine shekinah. The new Jerusalem “does not need the sun or the moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light, and the Lamb is its lamp” (Revelation 21:23).
28: Two additional frames for the back corners. The corner frames: the joining point of the back wall and side walls. Christ as the cornerstone (Ephesians 2:20; 1 Peter 2:6) is the structural element joining two otherwise separate walls into one unified building. The cornerstone determines the alignment of everything else.
29: Paired at the bottom and joined at the top by a single ring — so for both corners. The corner frames joined as one at both base and crown: completely united. Christ's atonement unifies at the base (the same silver-of-redemption foundation) and at the crown (the same gold glory). Paul's prayer for unity in Ephesians 4:3-6: one body, one Spirit, one Lord, one faith, one baptism, one God.
30: Eight frames in all, with sixteen silver sockets. The complete back wall standing on ransom money. Every part of the tabernacle structure, including the hidden back wall, stands on the same full redemption. Nothing in the church stands on any other foundation (1 Corinthians 3:11).
31: Five bars of acacia wood for each side, five for the back. The crossbars running horizontally through the frames, holding vertical members in lateral solidarity. The bars are the type of the horizontal bonds of the covenant community — the fellowship holding believers together: “they devoted themselves to the apostles' teaching and to fellowship, to the breaking of bread and to prayer” (Acts 2:42).
32: Five bars for the south side, five for the north side, five for the west end. The same bar-structure on every side: horizontal bonding is uniform throughout. The church's horizontal fellowship (with one another) mirrors the vertical relationship (with God): both sustained by the same covenant structure.
33: The middle bar to run from end to end at the center of the frames. The middle bar (ha-beriach ha-tichon) runs the entire length of the tabernacle through the center of every frame. Christ is the one who “holds all things together” (Colossians 1:17) — the cosmic middle bar running through the entire structure of creation and redemption, from first to last. He is “the Alpha and the Omega, the First and the Last” (Revelation 22:13).
34: The frames and bars overlaid with gold, the rings of gold as holders for the bars. The gold overlay on the acacia frames and bars: the two-natures type, explicit and repeated. Every structural member bears the same acacia-overlaid-with-gold construction — as every aspect of Christ's person unites the creaturely (wood) and the divine (gold). Colossians 2:9: “in Christ all the fullness of the Deity lives in bodily form.”
35: The veil (paroket) of blue, purple, and scarlet yarn and fine twisted linen, with cherubim skillfully worked into it. The innermost boundary — the veil separating the holy place from the most holy place. Hebrews 10:20 explicitly identifies this veil with Christ's flesh: “by a new and living way opened for us through the curtain, that is, his body.” The veil that separated humanity from the divine presence is Christ himself — and when his body was torn on the cross, the veil was torn (Matthew 27:51).
36: Four pillars of acacia wood for the veil, overlaid with gold, with gold hooks, in four silver sockets. The veil's pillars stand in silver sockets (redemption foundation), carry gold hooks (divine connection), are acacia overlaid with gold (the two natures). The structure supporting the veil that is Christ's body: standing on ransom silver, connected to the divine purpose by gold, the humanity of the mediator in whom the fullness of God dwells (Colossians 2:9).
37: The screen for the entrance to the tent, embroidered in blue, purple, and scarlet yarn and fine twisted linen. The entrance screen: the first threshold into the sacred space, made with the same colors as the inner veil. Jesus's claim: “I am the door; whoever enters through me will be saved” (John 10:9). The embroidered entrance is the type of Christ who is the beautiful, multi-colored way into the Father's presence.
38: Five pillars with their hooks, and their five bronze sockets. The entrance screen's five bronze sockets: the threshold of the holy place is set in judgment-metal. No one enters without reckoning with the holy God. Christ's cross is the judgment on which the new entrance to God stands (John 12:31-32: “now is the time for judgment on this world”; “I, when I am lifted up... will draw all people to myself”).
